trace.content: Is LK-99 actually a room-temperature ambient-pressure superconductor? Investigate.
I'll look up the original claim and then the replication attempts, and weigh them.

arguments.q: LK-99 room temperature superconductor original preprint

trace.content: Top hit: Lee, Kim et al., 'The First Room-Temperature Ambient-Pressure Superconductor' (arXiv:2307.12008, Jul 2023). Claims a modified lead-apatite (LK-99) shows superconductivity below 400 K at ambient pressure, citing a sharp resistivity drop and partial levitation.
The original preprint (arXiv:2307.12008) claims LK-99 is a room-temperature ambient-pressure superconductor, based on a resistivity drop and partial magnetic levitation.

Multiple groups failed to replicate superconductivity. A Peking University / Beihang team (arXiv:2308.05143) found no Meissner effect. A Lawrence Berkeley National Lab DFT study (Griffin, arXiv:2307.16892) showed flat bands are theoretically possible but does not establish superconductivity.

Sinha et al. and others report that the sharp resistivity drop near 380 K in LK-99 coincides with the first-order phase transition of a Cu2S (copper sulfide) impurity, which alone explains the apparent drop without superconductivity.
Replication failures (arXiv:2308.05143) report no Meissner effect, directly contradicting the original superconductivity claim.
The Cu2S impurity phase transition near 380 K accounts for the resistivity drop that the original preprint (arXiv:2307.12008) interpreted as superconductivity.
The partial 'levitation' is attributed to ferromagnetism/diamagnetism of the sample rather than the Meissner effect; samples balanced on an edge rather than floating freely.
The partial levitation in arXiv:2307.12008 is better explained by ordinary diamagnetism, not the Meissner effect, weakening the superconductivity interpretation.
Therefore the weight of evidence indicates LK-99 is most likely NOT a room-temperature superconductor; the observed signatures are explained by a Cu2S impurity and ordinary magnetism.
